summary:
The most significant development in US data center policy this week was Governor Janet Mills' veto of Maine's LD 307 on April 24, 2026 — blocking what would have been the first statewide data center moratorium in US history. The bill had passed the legislature (House 79-62, Senate 21-13) and would have banned facilities over 20 MW until November 2027. Mills indicated she would have signed if the bill included a carve-out for the planned Jay data center at the former Androscoggin Mill site, but the current House and Senate vote margins fall short of a two-thirds override threshold. In a parallel move, she signed LD 713 excluding data centers from Maine's business development tax incentives and announced plans for an executive order creating a study council. Locally, Bangor's City Council enacted a six-month moratorium on data centers around April 21, and local opposition in Maine shows no sign of abating.

Beyond Maine, the broader state-level pattern is active but largely unresolved. Virginia's April 23 special session recessed without a budget deal, with negotiations still deadlocked over the state's $1.9 billion data center sales tax exemption — the Senate wants to eliminate it starting January 2027, while Governor Spanberger and the House prefer keeping it with environmental conditions. New Jersey's A796/S731 passed both the Assembly (55-18) and Senate and awaits Governor Sherrill's signature; it would require data centers using 100+ MW to pay at least 85% of their service costs under a 10-year commitment. Michigan HB 4521 remains in committee, but local momentum continues to build with Ypsilanti's utility authority (YCUA) voting unanimously on April 22 to impose a 12-month moratorium on water and sewer services for new data centers — a novel mechanism now drawing national attention. North Carolina continued its rapid accumulation of local actions: Swain County enacted a 12-month moratorium on April 21, adding to Apex, Rowan County, Orange County, and others acting in April 2026 alone.

On the construction side, the buildout continues at unprecedented scale. The OpenAI Stargate Abilene campus (590 MW) is on track for full completion mid-2026. Meta's Hyperion campus in Richland Parish, Louisiana remains under active construction with ~3,700 workers, backed by a $27 billion joint venture and 10 planned gas-fired power plants (7.5 GW) via Entergy. Crusoe is building two new AI factory buildings adjacent to Stargate Abilene for Microsoft after OpenAI declined the expansion. xAI's Colossus 2 in Memphis filed a $659 million building permit for a new 312,000 sq ft facility in Q1 2026, and the company now claims ~2 GW total Colossus capacity. Applied Digital signed a $7.5 billion, 15-year hyperscaler lease for 300 MW at its Delta Forge 1 campus in Louisiana on April 23.

The political trajectory points toward incremental regulation rather than broad moratoriums. The Maine veto illustrates how governors are willing to block sweeping pauses while supporting targeted measures like tax exemption removal and utility cost protections. States like Washington (SB 5982) and South Dakota (SB 135) show the emerging middle path: requiring data centers to absorb their own grid costs and enabling local oversight, without stopping construction outright. As the 2026 legislative sessions close, the fight is shifting toward the handful of states — New York, Vermont, Wisconsin, Illinois — where broad moratorium or POWER-Act-style bills still have a path through the legislature.
